TextENGNew: The Houjou family was notorious—the one household that had stood against the entire village during the dam conflict.
Both husband and wife were hot-blooded by nature, and they had waged open, undisguised hostilities especially against the Sonozaki family.
The entire village, united in its opposition to the dam, had turned its cold regard on them—yet husband and wife had carried on with remarkable brazenness, as if water off a duck's back.
In their own way, they were a remarkably tenacious couple.
But the children who had to be dragged along for the ride must have suffered terribly.
Both Satoko-chan and Satoshi-kun had been subjected to the village's cold treatment simply by virtue of their parents' actions.
On top of that, there was the friction between Satoko-chan and her stepfather to contend with—their psychological stress must have been stretched to its absolute limit.
As the director of the clinic—if I may be so bold, something of a local notable—I was in a position to hear many things from many people.
...So I already knew how badly the two of them were being treated and how much they were suffering, even before we ever met.
My connection with the Houjou siblings began with Satoshi-kun, before Satoko-chan.
He had come to the clinic because the daily accumulation of stress had broken down his health.
He himself seemed at first to believe his symptoms were nothing more than a cold.
He said as much to me himself—that he thought it was probably a cold.
...But the frequency of his sighs, and those utterly exhausted eyes—
I sensed at once that Satoshi-kun's condition was not the product of something as simple as a cold.
Some medication could treat his immediate symptoms, certainly.
But that would only be a temporary patch.
A true cure would require addressing the living environment itself.
Easier said than done, of course.
...The web of circumstances surrounding him—the village, the family—was too tangled and interlocking. This was not the kind of thing that would clear up with a few days' rest and some medicine.
The village's hostility toward the Houjou name could probably only be dissolved by time—and there was no doubt it would take a very, very long time indeed.
...That was precisely why I felt I had to become his first ally in this village—to help him through it.
changeReason: Old translation used 'their' (plural) but Japanese says 彼を (him, singular, referring to Satoshi-kun); new translation correctly uses 'his.'

TextENGNew: If I couldn't remedy the environment producing the stress, the only option was to find some way to ease the stress he was receiving from it.
The best approach, I decided, was to teach him how to release it.
And nothing releases stress quite like regular physical exercise.
I invited him to join the youth baseball team I was coaching.
As a young researcher, I devoted the better part of my time to studying Hinamizawa Syndrome—but a man cannot live by research alone without growing despondent.
Having played baseball in my youth, I had taken on the role of coach for the local junior team.
...Before I knew it, the Hinamizawa children had split off to form their own team, and I found myself installed as their manager.
A physician's role is not merely to dispense medicine.
A sound mind dwells in a sound body.
Health can be cultivated through sport just as surely as through medicine.
The newly formed team was short on players, however.
...And that was when I thought of inviting Satoshi-kun.

Satoshi-kun was, by his own and others' admission, a literary type with little interest in sports, and he seemed genuinely puzzled as to how joining a baseball team could possibly help him breathe through his suffocating situation.
I had no need to lecture him at length on sports medicine to explain why physical activity relieves stress.
The best explanation was for him to break a sweat himself and feel the effect firsthand—nothing could beat that.
I believe he came to appreciate it gradually.
...Reluctant to the very last, perhaps—yet he showed up diligently for every team activity and match.
Watching Satoshi-kun gradually reclaim his vitality, I allowed myself a private moment of satisfaction that my approach had not been wrong.

Yet if Satoshi-kun was carrying this much stress,
then Satoko-chan, who shared the same home environment, must have been carrying exactly as much.
Shortsighted as I was, I had not thought of Satoko-chan at that time.
...So when Satoko-chan was brought before me as a patient, I was seized with bitter regret at having forgotten entirely about her circumstances.
changeReason: Old says 'doing nothing while knowing the situation' but JPN 失念していた means 'had forgotten entirely' — fundamentally changes the nature of his guilt from neglect to forgetfulness.

TextENGNew: While Satoshi-kun was gradually recovering under my guidance, Satoko-chan had been left behind alone in that toxic environment.
Around this same period, there was also an incident that would have been enough in itself to break Satoko-chan.
Their parents' accident.
changeReason: Old translation said 'parents' deaths' but Japanese says 両親の事故 (parents' accident); new translation uses 'accident' which is more accurate since this refers to the fall at the park, and also changes 'her' to 'their' matching the context of both siblings.

TextENGNew: In those days the Watanagashi was as much a celebration of victory in the dam conflict as it was a village festival, which made the day of the festival extremely uncomfortable for the Houjou family.
It was entirely understandable that they would plan a family trip to take them away from the village during that period.
It was at a park on that trip that Satoko-chan's parents fell to their deaths.
Truly, the worst of days for such an accident to occur.
...Because it had happened on the day of the Watanagashi, it would in time be passed down as another manifestation of Oyashiro-sama's curse.
And the Houjou family would be branded as a cursed household, marked by Oyashiro-sama's wrath...
It had been the parents themselves who drew the village's scorn.
changeReason: New translation clarifies that the Japanese specifies 両親だった (it was the parents) who were targeted, emphasizing the scorn was directed at the parents specifically—not the children—which is the key point of the passage.

TextENGNew: With the parents gone, the cold treatment should have ended along with them.
...But through truly terrible misfortune, the accident had occurred on the day of the Watanagashi...
The ill-treatment of the Houjou family would linger on without resolution, indefinitely...
Satoko-chan had been the only one present at the scene of the fall.
(Satoshi-kun had not gone on the trip because he was attending a baseball team event that day.)
As a result, the police questioning was directed entirely at Satoko-chan alone.
...The detective, Ooishi, was apparently convinced this was some form of organized crime by the village, and he bombarded Satoko-chan—the sole witness at the scene—with relentless questions.
She was carrying even more stress than Satoshi-kun—it would have been strange if all of this hitting her at once hadn't broken her.
During the interrogation, her emotions had overwhelmed her and she had gone into respiratory distress.
changeReason: Old translation omitted the emotional trigger (感情が高ぶり = emotions overwhelmed her) and only described the symptom; new translation includes both the cause and effect.

TextENGNew: The expression of anguish on Satoko-chan's face when she was brought in was something very, very hard to look upon.
Just trying to gauge how much suffering that slight, fragile body had absorbed was enough to make my chest ache.
"...Coach, is Satoko going to be all right...!"

"Yes, please don't worry.
She became temporarily overwhelmed, that's all."
"Is it all right if I see her?"

"She's only just managed to fall into a proper sleep with the help of medicine.
I'd rather not disturb her if we can help it.
Let's let her rest quietly for a while."
changeReason: Old translation omitted 薬で (with the help of medicine) which explains how Satoko managed to sleep; new translation includes this medical detail.

TextENGNew:
"Depending on how she progresses, it may be best to have her stay here for a few days.
...Don't worry.
Leave her to me."
"...Th-thank you."
I had always known that Satoshi-kun was a devoted and admirable older brother.
And that he possessed the sensitivity to share in his sister's inner pain.
...In other words, all of Satoshi-kun's anxieties and stress had been a mirror reflecting Satoko-chan.
He would never have said it aloud, knowing it was improper—but alongside his grief at his parents' deaths, there may have been a part of Satoshi-kun that felt relief.
changeReason: Old attributes the reluctance to speak to the narrator ('Maybe I shouldn't say this') when JPN attributes it to Satoshi — changes who is self-censoring, though the underlying observation about Satoshi's mixed feelings is preserved.

TextENGNew: Because now, his sister would no longer be driven into a corner.
Satoko-chan possessed none of Satoshi-kun's ability to navigate the world—to manage relations with a stepfather she shared no blood with.
So setting the mother aside, the removal of the father—the single greatest source of stress in Satoko-chan's life—was bound to bring a significant change to her circumstances.
...And yet.
...What followed was the two of them being placed with their aunt and uncle—an environment even worse than before...
At the time I had allowed myself the somewhat optimistic hope that the new living environment would be a gentler one for the siblings...
